meta.title: Composable vs Pinia store for a shared Vue product surface
meta.summary: A multi-screen Vue product surface has optimistic updates, background refreshes, draft restore, and several teams touching the same shared state graph. Would you keep the core logic in composables or move it into a Pinia store?

scenario: You are designing a Vue 3 claims workspace used by support and operations teams. The product includes a searchable queue, record details, side panels, staged edits, optimistic updates, websocket refreshes, retry flows for failed mutations, draft restore after route changes, and deep links back into the same record context. Several teams own different UI areas, but they all depend on the same core entity state and workflow transitions. In the interview, the panel asks whether you would keep the shared feature logic in composables or move the core shared state into a Pinia store.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Keep the core logic in composables
options.summary: Fits best when the shared logic is still bounded, feature ownership is clear, and reactive workflows can stay understandable without introducing a store-level convention layer.
whenItWins: The state mostly belongs to one route-bounded feature or one team, and the reactive workflow can stay coherent behind a focused composable API.
A small set of composables can model the feature cleanly without recreating a full event system in disguise.
The product does not yet need broad cross-screen coordination, persistent shared state rules, or a standardized debugging story across many contributors.
watchOutFor: As event sources multiply, composables can quietly turn into a custom store with implicit conventions and weaker traceability.
A weak answer says composables are lighter without explaining how optimistic rollback, background refresh, and cross-team debugging stay coherent over time.

options.label: Move the core shared state into Pinia
options.summary: Fits best when the feature behaves like a small product platform with long-lived shared state, many event sources, and a real need for stable conventions.
whenItWins: Several screens and teams depend on the same source of truth, and the product benefits from one clear place to own shared state, actions, and derived reads.
Optimistic updates, retries, websocket refreshes, route re-entry, and restore flows create enough event complexity that explicit store ownership becomes valuable.
The team needs a stronger debugging and maintenance story than a set of ad hoc shared composables can comfortably provide.
watchOutFor: Pinia is weaker when the feature is still modest and route-bounded, because the store boundary can add more structure than the current problem needs.
A weak answer says Pinia is always the Vue answer for serious apps without separating core shared state from local component or feature-level state.

decisionMatrix.title: Bounded feature with clear ownership
decisionMatrix.prompt: One team owns the workflow, the shared state surface is modest, and most coordination stays inside one feature boundary.

cells.note: This is easier to defend because a focused composable layer can stay explicit without paying store-wide structure too early.
Still viable, but you need to explain why the stronger store boundary already pays off before the feature has real platform pressure.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: Several teams touch the same entity graph, optimistic workflows exist, background updates race with user edits, and the product needs a stable shared-state story.

cells.note: Possible, but harder to defend because the composable layer now has to own a product-sized event model without a stronger shared-state convention.
This is easiest to defend because the product has enough cross-screen coordination and team pressure that explicit store ownership becomes part of the value.

decisionMatrix.title: Long-lived product surface with growing workflows
decisionMatrix.prompt: The feature keeps adding teams, route re-entry paths, side effects, and shared workflow rules over time.

cells.note: At this point the answer starts to sound optimistic unless the composables have already become store-like in everything but name.
The clearer shared-state boundary becomes easier to defend as the product surface and team count continue growing.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is this still one feature boundary or now a shared product surface?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the first question. Composables are excellent for bounded logic, but the answer changes when several screens and teams now share the same state graph.

evaluationDimensions.title: How many state transition sources exist?
evaluationDimensions.description: Optimistic writes, websocket refreshes, retries, deep-link re-entry, and draft restore all push the architecture toward a more explicit shared-state model.

evaluationDimensions.title: How will engineers explain and inspect state changes later?
evaluationDimensions.description: A senior answer should care about more than code volume. The real question is how reliably engineers can trace why state changed when several flows intersect.

evaluationDimensions.title: How many teams need the same mental model?
evaluationDimensions.description: As more contributors touch the same workflows, consistent state ownership and mutation conventions become more valuable.

evaluationDimensions.title: Can you keep the shared store narrow?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should avoid false binaries. Even if Pinia wins for core shared state, local view state and tiny component interactions should usually stay outside the store.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean Pinia because this is no longer just shared feature logic, it is a multi-screen product state surface
strongAnswer.summary: This claims workspace has enough cross-screen coordination, async event pressure, and multi-team ownership that shared state conventions become part of the product's operability. That makes Pinia the easier answer to defend for the core shared state.
reasoning: The key signal is not simply that Vue can share logic with composables. It is that several event sources now interact with the same entity graph: user edits, optimistic writes, websocket refreshes, retries, restore flows, and deep-link re-entry.

Composable-based architecture is still real and valuable. I would happily use it for bounded route features or focused reusable workflows. But in this prompt the composable layer starts drifting toward a custom store, and then the question becomes whether we want store-like complexity with store-level conventions or without them.

Pinia gives a stronger boundary for the core shared state here: one place to own shared entities, workflow actions, and derived reads, while still letting local UI details stay close to components. I would keep transient UI concerns such as panel-open flags, hover state, and tiny local form state out of the store.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would move the core shared workflow and entity state into Pinia while keeping local transient state in components or focused composables. I would choose composables alone instead only if the surface were more route-bounded, had fewer intersecting event sources, and stayed under one team's direct ownership.

interviewerPushback.question: Why not keep a disciplined set of composables and avoid a store?
interviewerPushback.answer: I would do that for a bounded feature. Here the issue is not just code size. Several teams and event sources now need the same shared-state model. At that point the discipline you need starts looking a lot like store architecture anyway, so I would rather adopt the clearer boundary deliberately.

interviewerPushback.question: Are you saying Pinia should own every piece of state?
interviewerPushback.answer: No. I would be very intentional about scope. Core shared workflow and entity state belong in the store here; local UI state such as panel toggles, hover state, and tiny component-only drafts should stay local.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the feature became more route-bounded, had fewer intersecting event sources, and one team could still reason about the whole state graph behind a focused composable API, I would move back toward composables as the primary boundary.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use Pinia because serious Vue apps should use a store.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds generic and misses the real boundary between bounded composable logic and a product-sized shared-state model.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use Pinia because the feature is complex and many teams are involved, although composables could still work.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs sharper reasoning about event sources, debugging, and why the composable boundary is no longer the cleaner model.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would move the core shared workflow state into Pinia because the workspace now has several event sources touching the same entity graph: optimistic user edits, websocket refreshes, retries, restore flows, and deep-link re-entry. That is the point where explicit shared store ownership becomes valuable as an operating model, not just as extra structure. I would still keep transient local UI state out of the store. If the feature were more route-bounded and owned by one team, I would be comfortable keeping it in focused composables instead.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it explains why Pinia wins for this exact product shape, avoids the 'store by default' trap, and still gives a clean boundary for when composables remain the better answer.

answerFramework: Start by naming whether the surface is still a bounded feature or now a shared product state surface.
List the event sources and workflow pressures that shape the state architecture.
Choose the model that best supports those transitions and debugging needs.
Be explicit about scope: what belongs in shared store state and what should stay local.
End with a calm boundary, for example: composables for bounded reusable workflows, Pinia for multi-screen event-heavy shared product state.
antiPatterns: Saying Pinia is automatically correct for any serious Vue app.
Saying composables are always simpler without accounting for event complexity and team scale.
Putting every tiny local UI flag into the store once Pinia is introduced.
Talking only about boilerplate instead of transition clarity, debugging, and ownership boundaries.
